title: Good: EUI-64 generation uses SHA-256 to handle MCU-specific hwid layouts
description:
lora_l2.c:generate_eui64() at lines 80-148 hashes the hardware ID rather than using raw bytes:

```c
/*
 * Hashes the full hardware ID using SHA-256 to produce a collision-resistant
 * EUI-64. This avoids issues with MCU-specific hwid layouts:
 * - STM32: 12-byte UID where bytes 0-3 are lot/wafer (shared across chips)
 * - ESP32: 6-byte MAC (would leave 2 bytes unfilled)
 * - nRF52: 8-byte unique ID
 *
 * By hashing the full hwid, we mix all available entropy bits uniformly.
 */
```

Lines 125-131 document the security of truncating SHA-256 to 64 bits for identifier (not key) use, citing RFC 7343 (ORCHID).

What goes right: The comment explains WHY hashing is needed (MCU layout issues), not just what it does. The collision probability analysis is documented. The code handles variable-length hwid input. The intermediate buffers are zeroed on all paths.

Worth defending: Using raw hwid bytes would be simpler but would fail on STM32 (lot numbers shared across chips) or ESP32 (only 6 bytes). The hash approach is the correct solution.
close_reason: Positive finding - pattern to preserve, not an issue
